title: Pretraining Before Fine-Tuning
concept: Many modern models first pretrain on broad data to learn general patterns, then fine-tune on a smaller labeled dataset so those representations become specialized for one downstream task.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which is a common self-supervised training trick?

options.text: Hide part of the input and predict it.
Replace the dataset with one hand-picked example.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Many self-supervised methods create prediction tasks by masking, shifting, or reconstructing part of the input.

cards.title: Foundation first, customization later
cards.body: When builders construct a house, they do not start with curtains and paint colors. They pour the foundation first. Modern AI often works the same way: learn broad reusable patterns first, then specialize for the exact job later.

cards.title: Pretraining and fine-tuning
cards.body: Pretraining is the broad first stage where a model learns general structure from lots of data, often with self-supervised tasks. Fine-tuning is the later stage where that already-trained model is adjusted for a specific task using a smaller labeled dataset.

cards.title: General schooling before a specialty
cards.body: Think of pretraining as a broad education where you build reading, reasoning, and pattern-recognition skills. Fine-tuning is like later training for one profession, such as medicine or law, where those general skills get pointed toward a narrower goal.

cards.title: Learn broadly, then adapt to medicine
cards.body: A vision model might pretrain on huge collections of ordinary images to learn edges, textures, shapes, and object structure. Later, a medical team fine-tunes it on a smaller labeled scan dataset so the model uses those general visual representations for a specialized clinical task.

cards.title: Pretraining does not finish the job
cards.body: A common myth is that once a model is pretrained, it is automatically perfect for every task. In reality, pretraining gives a strong starting point. Fine-tuning is what adapts that starting point to the exact labels, style, and goals of a downstream problem.

cards.title: Broad first pass vs. narrow final pass
cards.body: The two stages play different roles.

left.text:
Huge broad dataset
Learns general patterns and representations
Often self-supervised

right.text:
Smaller task dataset
Adapts the model to one goal
Often uses labels

exercises.prompt: Which step usually comes first in this pipeline?

options.text: Pretraining on broad data before task-specific fine-tuning.
Fine-tuning before the model has learned any broad patterns.
Deleting the pretrained model and starting over for every task.
Skipping all general learning and using labels only at inference time.

exercises.explain: Correct. The usual flow is broad learning first, then specialization later.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Fine-tuning usually exists to make a broadly trained model more specialized for one downstream task.
exercises.explain: True. Fine-tuning takes general representations and nudges them toward a narrower objective.

exercises.sentence: Pretraining builds general representations; fine-tuning makes them more task ___.
exercises.explain: Specific is correct. Fine-tuning adapts broad knowledge to one concrete job.

exercises.prompt: Which workflow best matches pretraining before fine-tuning?

options.text: Train on a huge general image collection, then adapt the model using a smaller labeled plant-disease dataset.
Collect one labeled image and claim the model now understands all images.
Use a pretrained model but refuse to update it for the target task at all, even when labels are available.

exercises.explain: Exactly. That is the classic broad-first, specialize-later workflow.

exercises.prompt: A hospital has millions of unlabeled scans but only a few thousand labeled ones for a diagnosis task. What is the best big-picture strategy?

options.text: Pretrain on the large scan collection to learn broad visual structure, then fine-tune on the smaller labeled diagnosis dataset.
Ignore the unlabeled scans because only manually labeled data can teach useful features.
Fine-tune first, then pretrain later on unrelated data.
Use the labeled scans as a validation set only and never train on them.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Pretraining can turn the huge unlabeled collection into useful representations, and fine-tuning then aligns those representations with the diagnosis labels.
